title: The letters and the language
steps.0.text: Add `<meta charset="utf-8">` as the first thing inside `<head>`.
checkLabels.t1: There is a meta with a charset
why.empty: The editor is empty. You are adding two lines, and you deleted the whole page. Bring the code back.
hint2.empty: Restore the skeleton from the last lesson and add the two things to the head and to `<html>`.
why.not-closed: Some tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
hint2.not-closed: `<meta>` has no closing tag — it is a void element. Check the others.
why.no-meta: No character encoding is declared. The browser will guess how to read your letters, and every letter outside the English alphabet becomes a gamble.
hint2.no-meta: The tag is `meta`. It goes in `<head>` and has no closing tag.
hint3.no-meta: The whole line is `<meta charset="utf-8">`. Look at the example above.
why.empty-charset: The encoding is declared but empty. An empty attribute passes every "is it written" check and says nothing. The browser still has to guess.
hint2.empty-charset: The value is `utf-8`. It goes inside the quotes.
why.charset-not-in-head: The encoding is there, but not in the head. It has to be among the first things in the file — the browser reads it before it reaches the text. Further down is already too late.
hint2.charset-not-in-head: Look at where you put it. Is it between `<head>` and `</head>`?
hint3.charset-not-in-head: It belongs as the first thing inside the head — before `<title>`.
why.no-lang: There is no `lang`. The screen reader does not know what language it is reading and will pronounce your text in the wrong voice.
hint2.no-lang: It is not a new tag. It is written INSIDE the opening `<html>`, before the closing bracket.
hint3.no-lang: The tag is already there — you are only adding something to it. Look at the example.
why.empty-lang: `lang` is there but empty. An empty attribute is the same as a missing one — nobody knows what the language is.
hint2.empty-lang: The value is the language code: `bg` for Bulgarian, `en` for English.
hint3.empty-lang: If your page is in English — `<html lang="en">`.
why.lost-doctype: The declaration from the last lesson is gone. You are adding things, not deleting old ones.
why.lost-content: Content disappeared from the page. The task is to add two lines, not to delete.
hint2.lost-content: Compare with the starting code. The body stays untouched.
blocks.1.text: Your page is written in some language, with some letters. You know that. The browser does not.
blocks.2.text: That raises two questions you have to answer: how to read your letters, and what language the words are in. They are different things and they take different lines.
blocks.4.text: A computer does not store letters. It stores numbers. To turn them back into letters it needs a table — which number is which letter. You tell it which one:
blocks.5.marks.0.label: INFORMATION
blocks.5.marks.1.label: ABOUT WHAT
blocks.5.marks.2.label: THE TABLE
blocks.5.legend.0: `meta` has no closing tag. It wraps nothing — it only carries a piece of information.
blocks.6.items.0: `meta` is the tag for information ABOUT the page. Hence its place — in the head.
blocks.6.items.1: `charset` is which set of characters is in use.
blocks.6.items.2: `utf-8` is the table that covers every writing system — Cyrillic, Chinese, Arabic, emoji. Around 98 per cent of pages on the web use it today.
blocks.8.text: The browser starts with a guess of its own, usually based on where the person is. If it guesses right, lucky. If not, you get that letter breakdown you have seen: question marks, boxes, strange glyphs. It even has a name — mojibake.
blocks.9.text: The classic example is `café` coming out as `cafÃ©`. Notice the page does not crash and no error appears anywhere. Your code works — it just lies about the letters.
blocks.10.text: Why it goes first in the head
blocks.11.text: The specification wants this line to fit within the first 1024 bytes of the file — counting the declaration, `<html>`, `<head>` and the line itself. The reason is reading order: the browser has to know the table before it starts decoding the text.
blocks.12.text: If it reaches a character it cannot decode, it has to start parsing over. Some browsers do it and lose time; others do not bother and leave the mess. That is why the line sits at the top of the head, before `<title>`, and not somewhere further down.
blocks.14.text: The encoding says how to read the letters. The language says what the words are. The second one is not a new tag — it is something written inside a tag you already have:
blocks.16.text: Something written inside an opening tag like this is called an *attribute*. It has a name on the left and a value in quotes on the right. There is a whole lesson on them shortly — for now it is enough to know where they go.
blocks.18.items.0: The screen reader. It needs to know how to pronounce the words. Without `lang` it will read Bulgarian text in an English voice and produce noise nobody understands.
blocks.18.items.1: Google. It decides who to show your page to. If it does not know the language, it shows it to the wrong people, or to nobody.
blocks.18.items.2: The browser's translator. It offers a translation only when it knows where to start from.
blocks.20.text: Add both: the encoding as the first thing in `<head>`, and the language on the opening `<html>`.
blocks.21.text: Run it. Nothing will change on screen — the third lesson in a row. If you are getting used to that, you have understood who the head is written for.
blocks.23.items.0: `<meta charset="utf-8">` says which table to read the letters by.
blocks.23.items.1: It goes first in `<head>`, before everything else.
blocks.23.items.2: Without it, letters outside the English alphabet fall apart.
blocks.23.items.3: `lang` on `<html>` says what language the words are in.
blocks.23.items.4: It is read by screen readers, by Google and by the translator.
walkthrough.0.text: Two lines added to the skeleton. Nothing old was touched.
walkthrough.1.text: The encoding is the first child of the head — before `<title>`. The standard does not demand that exact position, but it is the only way to be sure it fits inside the first 1024 bytes, whatever you add later.
walkthrough.2.text: `lang="en"` is for an English page. A Bulgarian one takes `bg`. The check does not look at which value you used, only that it is not empty: it cannot know what language you are writing in.
